coredata.dc:title: Unraveling the enhanced oxidative desulfurization activity: Synergistically boosting the formation of reactive oxygen species and active centers
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Tong, Shuang
Huang, Tianqi
Chen, Mengyue
Wang, Chao
Ji, Mengxia
Zhu, Linhua
Li, Huaming
Zhang, Ming
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) activation is a pivotal factor influencing the oxidative desulfurization efficiency. Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) material can be facilely functionalized through chemical modifications, thus effectively activating H2O2 for oxidative reactions. Herein, a kind of hybrid materials C-dots-modified polyoxometalate poly(ionic liquid) supported on silica dioxide (C-PIL-POM-SiO2) were successfully synthesized via a facile self-assembly method. The experimental results demonstrated that the doped C-dots facilitated electron transfer during the reaction, thereby accelerating the conversion of H2O2 into superoxide (·O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH). The incorporation of SiO2 significantly enhanced the specific surface area of the catalysts C-PIL-POM-SiO2, leading to abundant exposed active sites to boost the mass transfer during the reaction. The synergistic effects could achieve an optimal desulfurization efficiency of 99.9 % within 40 min.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Polyoxometalate poly(ionic liquid)
H2O2 activation
Oxidative desulfurization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of the PIL-POM, PIL-POM-SiO2, C-PIL-POM, and C-PIL-POM-SiO2; (b) FT-IR spectra of the HPW, PIL-POM-SiO2, and C-PIL-POM-SiO2; (c) Raman spectra of the C-dots, PIL-POM-SiO2, and C-PIL-POM-SiO2; (d) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of the PIL-POM, PIL-POM-SiO2, C-PIL-POM, and C-PIL-POM-SiO2.

TEM images of (a) C-dots (the insert image is UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra of CDs), (b) PIL-POM-SiO2, (c) C-PIL-POM-SiO2. (d) SEM image and the corresponding EDS elemental mappings of C-PIL-POM-SiO2, including W, Si, O, and C elements.

(a) Desulfurization efficiency of different catalysts. Experimental conditions: m (catalyst) = 10 mg, T = 50 °C, O/S = 3; (b) Desulfurization efficiency at varied reaction temperatures. Experimental conditions: m (catalyst) = 10 mg, O/S = 3; (c) Desulfurization efficiency with varied ratios of O/S. Experimental conditions: m (catalyst) = 10 mg, T = 50 °C; (d) Desulfurization efficiency with different amounts of catalysts. Experimental conditions: T = 50 °C, O/S = 3; (e) Desulfurization efficiency on different sulfides (Orange is 4, 6-DMDBT, Green is 4-MDBT, Purple is DBT). Experimental conditions: m (catalyst) = 10 mg, T = 50 °C, O/S = 3; (f) Recycling performance of C-PIL-POM-SiO2. Experimental conditions: m (catalyst) = 10 mg, T = 50 °C, O/S = 3. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis DRS of as-prepared C-dots, PIL-POM, PIL-POM-SiO2, C-PIL-POM, and C-PIL-POM-SiO2, (b) The Cyclic voltammograms curves of the as-prepared materials in 0.1 M KOH solutions, (c) DMPO-O2 − radical capture experiment; (d) DMPO-·OH radical capture experiment.
Scheme for the synthesis process of the C-PIL-POM-SiO2.
Proposed possible ODS mechanism of C-PIL-POM-SiO2.

head.ce:title: Unraveling the enhanced oxidative desulfurization activity: Synergistically boosting the formation of reactive oxygen species and active centers

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) activation is a pivotal factor influencing the oxidative desulfurization efficiency. Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) material can be facilely functionalized through chemical modifications, thus effectively activating H2O2 for oxidative reactions. Herein, a kind of hybrid materials C-dots-modified polyoxometalate poly(ionic liquid) supported on silica dioxide (C-PIL-POM-SiO2) were successfully synthesized via a facile self-assembly method. The experimental results demonstrated that the doped C-dots facilitated electron transfer during the reaction, thereby accelerating the conversion of H2O2 into superoxide (·O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH). The incorporation of SiO2 significantly enhanced the specific surface area of the catalysts C-PIL-POM-SiO2, leading to abundant exposed active sites to boost the mass transfer during the reaction. The synergistic effects could achieve an optimal desulfurization efficiency of 99.9 % within 40 min.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of automobile industries, the daily consumption of liquid fuels by vehicle has led to the emission of sulfur oxide (SOx), which has become one of the growing environmental problems [1,2]. Hence, governments have implemented stricter standards to limit the sulfur content in fuel to an ultra-low level [3,4]. Consequently, the exploration of deep desulfurization methods has become an urgent issue [5,6]. As traditional technology in petroleum refining industry, hydrodesulfurization (HDS) exhibits good desulfurization performance on aliphatic sulfide [7–11]. However, for aromatic sulfides including dibenzothiophene (DBT) and its derivatives, HDS requires more rigorous requirements for deep desulfurization, inevitably resulting in the increase of the production cost [12]. As one of supplemental technologies for HDS, oxidative desulfurization technology (ODS) has been explored due to its advantages such as deep desulfurization over aromatic sulfides and mild operating condition [13,14].

Polyoxometalates (POMs) are polyacid compounds with octahedral structural units, formed by linking two or more transition metal ions via oxygen atoms, which have a reversible multi-electron redox capacity [15]. POMs are widely applied in catalytic reactions due to their particular acidity, strong oxidizing properties, and good thermal stability [16]. Especially, the Keggin-type of POMs are favored for oxidative desulfurization due to their reversible multi-electron nature and excellent redox capability [17,18]. However, POMs exhibit strong hydrophilicity and low specific surface area, affecting their catalytic activity and cycle performance [19–21]. To overcome these limitations, POMs were immobilized to form heterogeneous catalysts, such as mesoporous silica [22], carbon materials [23,24], and metal–organic frameworks [25]. Nevertheless, the interaction between the active sites of the heterogeneous catalyst and the porous material is relatively weak compared to homogeneous catalysts [26]. It has been demonstrated that catalysts prepared by loading heteropolyacids on mesoporous silica can effectively increase the specific surface area, thus facilitating the ODS process [27]. Furthermore, polyionic liquids (PIL) are polymers with anion and cation electrolyte groups in their repeating units, exhibiting strong polarity, thermal stability, structural designability, and tunable physical and chemical properties [28,29]. PIL can attach POMs to the cationic polymer backbone through anion exchange, accelerating mass and energy transfer on the boundary surface [30]. PIL have been widely used in various areas, including porous materials [31], sorbent materials [32], and electrochemical materials [33,34]. Polyoxometalate polyionic liquids (POM-PIL) are hybrid materials based on POMs, combining the inherent redox and catalytic properties of POMs with the unique mobility of conventional PIL [35,36]. Thus POM-PIL can serve as both solvents and catalysts, attracting widespread interest in the field of catalytic oxidation [37–39].

As an oxidation reaction, sufficient activation of the oxidant is considered to be a crucial step for the ODS process [40,41]. Various kind of oxidants are usd in ODS process, including tert-butyl hydroperoxide, ozone, molecular oxygen (O2), and H2O2 [42,43]. Among them, H2O2 is considered as an environmentally friendly oxidant due to its advantages, including low cost, high oxidative activity, and a neutral by-product (H2O) [44,45]. In recent years, carbon quantum dots (C-dots) is considered as a novel class of carbon nanomaterials with a diameter below 10 nm, attracting considerable attention for their advances, including the diverse synthetic sources, straightforward synthesis methodologies, low toxicity, robust chemical stability, and easy surface modifications [46,47]. Notably, abundant functional groups on the surface of C-dots make them attractive candidates for various applications [48]. The incorporation of C-dots into catalytic systems has been shown to significantly enhance the efficiency of H2O2, highlighting their potential as powerful additives in catalysis [49,50]. To date, few studies have focused on applying carbon dot-doped POM-PIL in ODS process.

In this study, the C-dots doped the PIL-POM-immobilized-SiO2 (C-PIL-POM-SiO2) catalysts were synthesized for ODS process. The characterizations demonstrated that the specific surface area of the samples was greatly increased by immobilizing PIL-POM on SiO2, thus facilitating the effective contact between the active sites and the sulfides. Moreover, the introduction of C-dots could boost the H2O2 activation, thereby improving the reaction rate and enhancing the activity stability of the catalyst C-PIL-POM-SiO2. Consequently, the ODS efficiency of the prepared samples was significantly enhanced.

Raman spectroscopy was further employed to demonstrate the successful complexation of C-dots. As can be observed in Fig. 1c, the two apparent peaks located at 990 and 1005 cm−1 in PIL-POM-SiO2 and C-PIL-POM-SiO2 are attributed to the Keggin structure of HPW, illustrating that the structure of the modified catalysts still remain intact. Moreover, the Raman peak at 1500 cm−1 in the C-PIL-POM-SiO2 corresponds to the CO group of C-dots, which matches well with the FT-IR results. To explore the changes in the pore structure and specific surface area after the introduction of SiO2 and C-dots, different samples were tested by Brunner-Emmet-Teller (BET) analysis. It can be seen that the PIL-POM-SiO2 and C-PIL-POM-SiO2 exbibit a type IV isotherm from the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (Fig. 1d). The BET surface area of the C-PIL-POM-SiO2 catalysts is much higher than that of the C-PIL-POM, which is conducive to improve the high dispersion of C-dots, thus ensuring the sufficient exposure of active centers. Meanwhile, compared to the C-PIL-POM (Table S1), the significant increase of average pore size and total pore volume of C-PIL-POM-SiO2 further affirms that the immobilization of PIL-POM on SiO2 could provide a favorable mass transfer channel for ODS reactions. The above results demonstrate that the immobilization of PIL-POM and C-PIL-POM on SiO2 is an effective strategy to enhance the contact between the catalysts and sulfides, thereby boosting ODS activity.

The morphology of various samples was analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. The diameter of prepared C-dots is measured to be 6 nm in Fig. 2 a. For fluorescence experiments (Fig. S5), the C-dots exhibits strong luminescence properties, further demonstrating the successful synthesis of C-dots. As depicted in the UV diffuse reflectance spectra and steady-state spectra (the inset image in Fig. 2a), the maximum emission wavelength (Em) of C-dots is 445 nm under the excitation wavelength (Ex) of 360 nm. There are two absorption bands at 275 and 360 nm observed in the C-dots, which respectively belong to the π–π* leaps of the CC bond and the n–π* leaps of the CO bond [52]. The photoluminescence intensity of C-dots directly relates to the Ex (Fig. S6). The C-dots exhibits a higher intensity when the n–π* band is excited. Instead, the intensity decreases when the excitation occurs in the far-ultraviolet range, especially in the π–π* absorption band. In Fig. S3, the pristine PIL-POM exhibits a lumpy structure, comprising aggregated flakes of varying sizes. The morphology structure does no change after the introduction of SiO2 and C-dots (Fig. S4). The details of the PIL-POM-SiO2 and C-PIL-POM-SiO2 are further observed via TEM analysis. Compared to PIL-POM-immobilized SiO2 sample (Fig. 2b), doping C-dots apparently improves the high dispersion of the PIL-POM (Fig. 2c), which facilitates providing rich catalytic centers for the deep ODS. The EDS elemental mapping analysis of the C-PIL-POM-SiO2 in Fig. 2d shows that the C, O, Si, and W elements are uniformly distributed. The effect of adding C-dots on the elemental compositions and valence states for the synthesized PIL-POM-SiO2 and C-PIL-POM-SiO2 samples was further investigated by XPS analysis. According to Figs. S7 and S8, the characteristic peaks of W6+ and W5+ can be found in the peak-differentiation-imitating W 4f XPS spectra of both PIL-POM-SiO2 and C-PIL-POM-SiO2, which indicates that the addition of C-dots does not change the valence state of W.

The desulfurization efficiency over DBT-containing model oil was performed to explore the effects of immobilization treatment and C-dots modification on under optimal reaction conditions. In Fig. 3 a, the pristine PIL-POM exhibits almost no desulfurization activity, while the introduction of C-dots could obviously boost the ODS reaction rate. However, the sulfur removal of the C-PIL-POM is still lower than 50 % within 60 min. For PIL-POM-SiO2, complete sulfur removal (100 %) could be achieved within 60 min after the immobilization of HPW and 1-vinyl-3-octylimidazole bromide on the SiO2, strongly indicating that the exposure of ample active sites is crucial for the ODS reaction. Furthermore, the catalyst C-PIL-POM-SiO2 exhibits the optimal desulfurization efficiency of more than 99.9 % of DBT within 40 min after the C-dots modification. However, the sulfur removal of PIL-POM-SiO2 only can reach 79.2 %. The apparent enhancement in desulfurization activity of the catalyst C-PIL-POM-SiO2 can be attributed to the significant increase of specific surface area, which is conducive to the full contact between the uniform dispersion of active sites and the sulfides. Moreover, the C-dots with abundant functional groups serve as a co-catalyst to effectively activate H2O2, thereby synergistically improving the catalytic performance. In addition, a series of parallel experiments were also performed over the catalyst C-PIL-POM-SiO2 (Fig. 3b–d). The optimal reaction conditions can be obtained as follows: m (catalyst) = 10 mg, T = 50 °C, O/S = 3.

To further study the desulfurization performance of the as-prepared catalysts with different sulfides, the desulfurization efficiencies of DBT, 4-MDBT, and 4,6-DMDBT were carried out under the same conditions. As depicted in Fig. 3e, the desulfurization rate of DBT, 4-MDBT, and 4,6-DMDBT can respectively reach 100.0 %, 96.9 %, and 94.9 % after 60 min. Herein, the desulfurization activity is primarily influenced by steric hindrance in the sulfides. Compared with 4-MDBT and 4,6-DMDBT, DBT is lack of methyl group in the benzene ring, resulting in the lower steric hindrance, which is beneficial for the ODS process [53,54]. The activity stability and reusability of the C-PIL-POM-SiO2 catalyst were further evaluated through cycling tests (Fig. 3f). The sulfur removal can still reach 97.8 % after 6 cycles, indicating the good reusability of the sample C-PIL-POM-SiO2. To further analyze the stability of the catalyst, the recovered C-PIL-POM-SiO2 was characterized. The catalyst exhibits excellent desulfurization benefits and stability in comparison with previous studies (Table S2). As shown in Fig. S9, the characteristic structure of C-PIL-POM-SiO2 remains unchanged after the ODS reaction. In addition, the observed additional peaks at 565, 757, 1161, and 1287 cm−1 are associated with the DBTO2 compound. This confirms that the catalyst exhibits excellent stability and that DBTO2 is formed during the ODS reaction. Additionally, the FTIR spectra of the model oil after reaction were also characterized (Fig. S10). The characteristic peaks ascribed to the POM is not observed in the model oil after reaction, suggesting that the sample C-PIL-POM-SiO2 was not dissolved in the oil. These findings demonstrate that the C-PIL-POM-SiO2 catalyst exhibits high structural stability and active stability for ODS process.

To study the reaction mechanism, the prepared samples were analyzed using ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis). Fig. 4 a reveals a distinct characteristic absorption peak in the 200–300 nm region for both C-PIL-POM-SiO2 and PIL-POM-SiO2. This peak is attributed to the electron transfer of the W atom in the center of the POM structure (O2− → W6+), indicating the successful introduction of POM onto SiO2. Additionally, shoulder peaks within the range of 350–500 nm are observed exclusively in C-PIL-POM-SiO2, which are ascribed to the absorption peaks of the C-dots. The incorporation of C-dots reduces the energy barrier for electronic excitation of the catalyst, thus facilitating the oxidative desulfurization process. To assess the redox capacity of the prepared samples, cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were plotted in Fig. 4b. The CV curves display a magnitude order of current changes as follows: C-PIL-POM-SiO2 > PIL-POM-SiO2 > C-PIL-POM. Notably, C-PIL-POM-SiO2 exhibits the highest cathodic and anodic currents, suggesting that the optimal electron mobility accelerates the oxidative desulfurization process.

Further investigation on the reaction mechanism was conducted through radical trapping experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) tests to identify the primary active intermediates during the reaction. In the radical trapping experiments (Fig. S11), 20 mmol/L of benzoquinone (BQ) and tert-butanol (TBA) were respectively utilized to scavenge ·O2 − and ·OH. A significant reduction in the desulfurization rate indicates the ·O2 − and ·OH are primary active intermediates in the ODS reaction. The ESR profiles of ·O2 − and ·OH capture were collected by using 5,5′-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as a trapping agent. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, the hexagonal peak DMPO-·O2 − and the quadruple peak DMPO-·OH can be observed in the presence of C-PIL-POM-SiO2 and H2O2, while no signals are detected without the addition of sample. These findings confirm that ·O2 − and ·OH are the main reactive oxygen radicals during the ODS reaction. To probe the reaction products of the ODS process, GC–MS analysis was performed on both the oil phase and the extracted phase of the catalyst after reaction (Fig. S12). A distinct peak corresponding to DBT (m/z = 184) can be observed before reaction, while only the peak for the internal standard (n-tetradecane) remains after reaction, indicating the complete removal of DBT. For the catalyst phase after reaction, an intense peak at 11.6 min corresponding to DBTO2 (m/z = 216) can be found, indicating the oxidation of DBT to DBTO2 (Fig. S13). Based on the above results, the doping of C-dots provides the PIL-POM catalyst with additional active sites for activating H2O2 to produce more ·O2 − and ·OH. Furthermore, the oxidative capacity of the catalyst is augmented, accelerating the conversion of DBT to the more easily extractable DBTO2 for effective desulfurization (Scheme 2 ).

To sum up, a kind of hybrid materials polyoxometalate poly(ionic liquid)-immobilized SiO2 doped with carbon quantum dots is successfully fabricated via a facile self-assembly method, and employed in the oxidative desulfurization of aromatic sulfides. The characterization results demonstrate that the incorporation of SiO2 can not only increase the specific surface area of the sample but also promote a more uniform distribution of active sites. Besides, the additional modification of C-dots, which possess abundant functional groups, can significantly enhance the utilization efficiency of H2O2. The synergistic effects can lead to outstanding oxidative desulfurization performance of the C-PIL-PM-SiO2, which can achieve a sulfur removal of 99.9 % over dibenzothiophene (500 ppm) in 40 min. This approach offers a new strategy for designing multifunctional polymeric metal oxide-based catalysts, surpassing previous studies in terms of efficiency, selectivity and cycling stability on the removal of various sulfides from oil. For future studies, the prepared samples will be optimized for other catalytic reactions, thus expanding its potential in sustainable and efficient process.

Shuang Tong: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Tianqi Huang: Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mengyue Chen: Investigation, Data curation. Chao Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Mengxia Ji: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Formal analysis, Data curation. Linhua Zhu: Visualization, Supervision, Resources. Huaming Li: Supervision, Resources. Ming Zhang: Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22108108, 22108105), Key Research and Development Project of Hainan Province (ZDYF2022GXJS330), Zhenjiang Science &Technology Program (GY2024032).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2025.137268.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Balula, Large-pore silica spheres as support for samarium-coordinated undecamolybdophosphate: Oxidative desulfurization of diesels, Fuel, 259 (2020) 116213.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Brønsted acidic SILP-based catalysts with H3PMo12O40 or H3PW12O40 in the oxidative desulfurization of fuels

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amphiphilic catalysts comprising phosphomolybdic acid fastened on MIL-101(Cr): enabling efficient oxidative desulfurization under solvent-free and moderate reaction conditions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhang, H. Wu, Y. Yesire, Y. Zhang, J. Ding, Y. Fan, M. Zhang, C. Wang, H. Li, Amphiphilic Catalysts Comprising Phosphomolybdic Acid Fastened on MIL-101(Cr): Enabling Efficient Oxidative Desulfurization under Solvent-Free and Moderate Reaction Conditions, Energy Fuels, 38 (2024) 8553−8563.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile construction of supported polyoxometalate ionic liquids for deep oxidative desulfurization of fuel

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Tong, T. Huang, M. Chen, Z. Zhu, C. Wang, H. Li, M. Zhang, Facile Construction of Supported Polyoxometalate Ionic Liquids for Deep Oxidative Desulfurization of Fuel, Catalysts, 14 (2024) 796.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrodesulfurization of methanethiol over Co-promoted MoS2 model catalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. K. Prabhu, J. N. Louwen, E. T. C. Vogt, I. M. N. Groot, Hydrodesulfurization of methanethiol over Co-promoted MoS2 model catalysts, Nat. Commun., 15 (2024) 7170.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of fluorescence-SERS dual-probe nanocomposites for ultrasensitive detection of sulfur-containing gases in water and beer samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Zhu, S. Ali, T. Jiao, Z. Wang, Y. Xu, Q. Ouyang, Q. Chen, Facile synthesis of fluorescence-SERS dual-probe nanocomposites for ultrasensitive detection of sulfur-containing gases in water and beer samples, Food Chem., 420 (2023) 136095.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic liquid promotes high dispersion of V2O5 on 3D porous g-C3N4 carrier to enhance catalytic oxidative desulfurization performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Xun, R. Le, C. Hu, D. Liu, S. Wang, M. He, W. Zhu, H. Li, Ionic Liquid Promotes High Dispersion of V2O5 on 3D Porous g-C3N4 Carrier to Enhance Catalytic Oxidative Desulfurization Performance, Energy Fuels, 37 (2023) 6276−6280.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of water-stable rare-earth organic frameworks with ambient high proton conductivity based on zirconium sandwiched heteropolytungstate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. H. Fan, M. Du, Y. X. Li, W. J. Zhu, J. Y. Pang, Y. Bai, D. B. Dang, Construction of Water-Stable Rare-Earth Organic Frameworks with Ambient High Proton Conductivity Based on Zirconium Sandwiched Heteropolytungstate, Inorg. Chem., 61 (2022) 13829−13835.
